1: <header>Fluidized bed combustion of poultry litter at farm-scale: Environmental impacts using a life cycle approach </header>
Abhinav Choudhury a, 1 Gary Felton Jonathan Moyle b, Stephanie Lansing a, *

<list>a University of Maryland, Department of Environmental Science and Technology, University of Maryland Energy Research Center, 1429 Animal Science/Ag Engineering Bldg., College Park, MD, 20742, USA 
b University of Maryland, Lower Eastern Shore Research and Education Center (LESREC), 27664 Nanticoke Road, Salisbury, MD, 21801-1648, USA</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 7 February 2020 Received in revised form 6 September 2020 Accepted 13 September 2020 Available online 18 September 2020 Handling editor: Prof. Jiri Jaromir Klemes Keywords: Waste-to-energy Assessment Environmental impact Emissions Sustainability LCA|"Combustion can concentrate phosphorus (P) from poultry litter into an ash product that is easier to transport for land application. This was the first life cycle assessment (LCA) study to investigate the efficacy and sustainability of a fluidized bed combustion (FBC) system using poultry litter to heat poultry houses and produce electricity, in the United States, to replace liquid propane gas (LPG) and natural gas (NG) use. The 'Baseline scenario"" used results from a 16-month FBC monitoring study, and an 'Improved scenario"" based on an increased biomass feed rate (0.246 tons/h), increased run-time (6720 h/y), and net positive electricity production. In the Baseline scenario, climate change potential was 32% and 44% lower than use of LPG and NG, respectively, for poultry house heating, but the low electricity production from the FBC system resulted in net electricity import and lower sustainability compared to LPG use in 12 of the 18 impact categories. The Improved scenario had 48-98% less environmental impacts than the Baseline scenario in the 18 categories. The sensitivity analysis showed that a 10% change in the electricity input for FBC operation resulted in the highest average change (4.8%) in the 18 impact categories, indicating the necessity of a net positive electrical energy output from the FBC unit for increased sus- tainability. The results also highlighted the impact of the replacement heating fuel type, as replacing NG through poultry litter combustion had a greater impact than replacement of LPG. © 2020 The Authors. 2: Poultry litter includes a mixture of manure and the bedding material, including wood chips, waste feed, and feathers, removed from poultry houses. Poultry litter has high concentrations of ni- trogen (N) (2.9-4.4%), phosphorus (P) (3.2-5.5%), and potassium (K) (2.2-3.8%) and is used as an organic fertilizer to recycle the nutrients back to agricultural fields (Chastain et al., 2012; De Filippis et al., 2014; Kelleher et al., 2002). The US poultry industry is estimated to generate in excess of 9 million tons of poultry litter each year (Hassanein et al., 2019). Direct spreading of poultry litter on agricultural farms located on the eastern shore in the state of Maryland in the United States (US) has resulted in P-enriched soils

(Codling, 2006). Poultry litter contains plant-available N:P of less than 2:1, while the crops grown on the Eastern Shore of Maryland require five times more N than P (on a mass basis) (Staver and Brinsfield, 2001). As a result, land application of poultry litter as an N source results in 3-4 times higher application of P than crops require. Leaching of nutrients from these soils over the years has contributed to the eutrophication of the Chesapeake Bay (Kleinman et al., 2019).

The heating value of poultry litter ranges from 9000 to 13,500 kJ/kg, depending on the material utilized and the moisture content, which is approximately half the heating value of coal (Dalólio et al., 2017; Kelleher et al., 2002). While there are chal- lenges to poultry litter combustion, poultry litter has a high energy content and produces an ash product that can be used as a P and K fertilizer source, making it a valuable resource. Commercial com- bustion of poultry litter to produce heat and electrical energy has been previously implemented in both Europe and the US (Qian et al., 2019). Additionally, there is a growing interest in combust- ing poultry litter to provide on-farm heating requirements due to increasing liquid propane gas (LPG) prices, greenhouse gas (GHG)

emissions associated with propane combustion, and eutrophication of water bodies due to leaching of nutrients from land application of poultry litter in the US. Fluidized bed combustion (FBC) tech- nology can be used to produce heat and produce electricity from poultry litter, either by itself or mixed with other domestic or in- dustrial wastes, due to its ability to handle low-grade fuels (Tanczuk et al., 2019).

A manure-to-energy system that generates heat and electricity using an FBC process was investigated in this study. In this process, the thermal energy generated was used to heat the poultry houses, with excess heat used in an organic Rankine cycle (ORC) device to produce electricity. The ORC turbine is an advanced power gener- ation technology that can convert low-grade heat into electricity and uses organic chemicals with low critical temperature and pressure, low specific volume, low viscosity and surface tension, and high thermal conductivity as working fluids (Ingrao et al., 2019; Cotana et al., 2014).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is an evaluation of environmental impacts through a systematic, inclusive, and analytical approach for any product or service (Horne et al., 2009). An LCA is most commonly used in comparing the environmental impact of a product or service with a comparable alternative to determine which product has a lower environmental impact. Billen et al. (2015) conducted an LCA of poultry litter combustion and concluded that surplus electricity production from poultry litter utilization prevented production of 655 kg CO2/ton poultry litter due to avoided impacts from coal combustion. Williams et al. (2016) conducted an LCA of turkey litter combustion and found reductions in energy demand (14%), eutrophication potential (55%), and acidification potential (70%) compared to its direct use as a fertilizer. These prior studies were conducted following European regulations, where poultry litter is managed differently than in the US. European regulations require poultry barns to have cement floors and to be cleaned out completely after each flock is removed and replaced with fresh bedding material (Jeswani et al., 2019). In poultry houses in Maryland, US, the litter is deposited on top of dirt or clay floors, and only part of the poultry litter is removed after each flock (crust out), thus, the composition of the poultry litter can be different after each flock (Kryzanowski, 2017). The cleanout of the entire poultry house occurs every one to three years. This study quantified the use of this type of poultry litter to replace LPG use for heating the poultry houses and the benefits of electricity produc- tion from the FBC unit. LPG is the common form for heating utilized in the US and can be up to 60% of the annual farm energy costs (Izar- Tenorio et al., 2020; Linhoss et al., 2017). Natural gas (NG) replacement fuel was also analyzed to determine the effect of the replacement fuel type on environmental impacts of using FBC technology.

The aim of this study was to quantify the environmental impacts of combusting poultry litter for energy generation and utilization. The LCA was used to evaluate and track the effectiveness of the FBC technology in reducing the environmental impacts when compared to the use of LPG and NG for heating poultry houses. The specific objectives were to: (1) quantify the environmental impacts of a full- scale FBC process based on a 16-month study of the material use, emissions, and energy production with a biomass feed rate of 0.176 tons/h, run-time of 3226 h, and net negative electricity production (parasitic load > electricity production), and compare the impacts to LPG and NG use, and (2) quantify the impacts associated with improved operational conditions, consisting of an increased biomass feed rate (0.246 tons/h), increased run-time (6720 h/y), and net positive electricity production (parasitic load < electricity production). While LPG was the primary replacement fuel in the study, replacing NG was also added to the model to assess the importance of the replacement fuel source type. The study results
can be used to understand sustainability based on the process conditions used for the combustion of organic substrates.

can be used to understand sustainability based on the process conditions used for the combustion of organic substrates.
7: Poultry litter includes a mixture of manure and the bedding material, including wood chips, waste feed, and feathers, removed from poultry houses. Poultry litter has high concentrations of ni- trogen (N) (2.9-4.4%), phosphorus (P) (3.2-5.5%), and potassium (K) (2.2-3.8%) and is used as an organic fertilizer to recycle the nutrients back to agricultural fields (Chastain et al., 2012; De Filippis et al., 2014; Kelleher et al., 2002). The US poultry industry is estimated to generate in excess of 9 million tons of poultry litter each year (Hassanein et al., 2019). Direct spreading of poultry litter on agricultural farms located on the eastern shore in the state of Maryland in the United States (US) has resulted in P-enriched soils

(Codling, 2006). Poultry litter contains plant-available N:P of less than 2:1, while the crops grown on the Eastern Shore of Maryland require five times more N than P (on a mass basis) (Staver and Brinsfield, 2001). As a result, land application of poultry litter as an N source results in 3-4 times higher application of P than crops require. Leaching of nutrients from these soils over the years has contributed to the eutrophication of the Chesapeake Bay (Kleinman et al., 2019).

The heating value of poultry litter ranges from 9000 to 13,500 kJ/kg, depending on the material utilized and the moisture content, which is approximately half the heating value of coal (Dalólio et al., 2017; Kelleher et al., 2002). While there are chal- lenges to poultry litter combustion, poultry litter has a high energy content and produces an ash product that can be used as a P and K fertilizer source, making it a valuable resource. Commercial com- bustion of poultry litter to produce heat and electrical energy has been previously implemented in both Europe and the US (Qian et al., 2019). Additionally, there is a growing interest in combust- ing poultry litter to provide on-farm heating requirements due to increasing liquid propane gas (LPG) prices, greenhouse gas (GHG)

* Corresponding author. E-mail addresses: abhinavc@terpmail.umd.edu (A. Choudhury), gfelton@umd. edu (G. Felton), jmoyle@umd.edu (J. Moyle), slansing@umd.edu (S. Lansing). 1 Current address: The Conservation Fund Freshwater Institute, 1098 Turner Road, Shepherdstown, WV 25443.achoudhury@conservationfund.org

emissions associated with propane combustion, and eutrophication of water bodies due to leaching of nutrients from land application of poultry litter in the US. Fluidized bed combustion (FBC) tech- nology can be used to produce heat and produce electricity from poultry litter, either by itself or mixed with other domestic or in- dustrial wastes, due to its ability to handle low-grade fuels (Tanczuk et al., 2019).

A manure-to-energy system that generates heat and electricity using an FBC process was investigated in this study. In this process, the thermal energy generated was used to heat the poultry houses, with excess heat used in an organic Rankine cycle (ORC) device to produce electricity. The ORC turbine is an advanced power gener- ation technology that can convert low-grade heat into electricity and uses organic chemicals with low critical temperature and pressure, low specific volume, low viscosity and surface tension, and high thermal conductivity as working fluids (Ingrao et al., 2019; Cotana et al., 2014).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is an evaluation of environmental impacts through a systematic, inclusive, and analytical approach for any product or service (Horne et al., 2009). An LCA is most commonly used in comparing the environmental impact of a product or service with a comparable alternative to determine which product has a lower environmental impact. Billen et al. (2015) conducted an LCA of poultry litter combustion and concluded that surplus electricity production from poultry litter utilization prevented production of 655 kg CO2/ton poultry litter due to avoided impacts from coal combustion. Williams et al. (2016) conducted an LCA of turkey litter combustion and found reductions in energy demand (14%), eutrophication potential (55%), and acidification potential (70%) compared to its direct use as a fertilizer. These prior studies were conducted following European regulations, where poultry litter is managed differently than in the US. European regulations require poultry barns to have cement floors and to be cleaned out completely after each flock is removed and replaced with fresh bedding material (Jeswani et al., 2019). In poultry houses in Maryland, US, the litter is deposited on top of dirt or clay floors, and only part of the poultry litter is removed after each flock (crust out), thus, the composition of the poultry litter can be different after each flock (Kryzanowski, 2017). The cleanout of the entire poultry house occurs every one to three years. This study quantified the use of this type of poultry litter to replace LPG use for heating the poultry houses and the benefits of electricity produc- tion from the FBC unit. LPG is the common form for heating utilized in the US and can be up to 60% of the annual farm energy costs (Izar- Tenorio et al., 2020; Linhoss et al., 2017). Natural gas (NG) replacement fuel was also analyzed to determine the effect of the replacement fuel type on environmental impacts of using FBC technology.

The aim of this study was to quantify the environmental impacts of combusting poultry litter for energy generation and utilization. The LCA was used to evaluate and track the effectiveness of the FBC technology in reducing the environmental impacts when compared to the use of LPG and NG for heating poultry houses. The specific objectives were to: (1) quantify the environmental impacts of a full- scale FBC process based on a 16-month study of the material use, emissions, and energy production with a biomass feed rate of 0.176 tons/h, run-time of 3226 h, and net negative electricity production (parasitic load > electricity production), and compare the impacts to LPG and NG use, and (2) quantify the impacts associated with improved operational conditions, consisting of an increased biomass feed rate (0.246 tons/h), increased run-time (6720 h/y), and net positive electricity production (parasitic load < electricity production). While LPG was the primary replacement fuel in the study, replacing NG was also added to the model to assess the importance of the replacement fuel source type. The study results
can be used to understand sustainability based on the process conditions used for the combustion of organic substrates.
8: An FBC system for poultry litter combustion was installed in Rhodesdale, MD, and was designed to produce 600 kW of heat for the poultry houses and 65 kW of electrical energy. The FBC system performance was quantified for 16 months in terms of energy generation, biomass consumption, ash production, time of opera- tion, and litter and ash characteristics. After each poultry flock, poultry litter that was removed from the barns was stored for FBC operation.

The litter was stored in a covered storage shed before being fed into the FBC system using a sensor-controlled scraping system connected to a conveyor belt. Inside the FBC system, multiple streams of hot air were used to suspend the fuel particles that were combusted within the furnace. The fluidization of the particles leads to an increase in surface area due to the constant turbulence and breaking up of larger particles into smaller sizes (Billen et al., 2015). This increased surface area leads to improved contact be- tween the particles and oxygen in the air. The ash produced from the combustion process was transferred to sealed bags for transport off-farm for use as a soil amendment.

The thermal energy generated by the combustion of the poultry litter was used to heat the poultry houses and replace LPG/NG usage. Excess heat was used to power the ORC for electricity pro- duction without additional fuel use or emissions. The electrical energy was used on-farm and/or to power the FBC conveyors and electrical components. During this 16-month study, the electrical energy required to operate the system (parasitic load) was higher than the electrical energy generated.

The poultry litter was analyzed for total %N at Agrolabs Inc. (Harrington, DE, US) using combustion analysis in an LECO 628 elemental analyzer (LECO, St. Joseph, MI, US), as described in A3769 Method for manure analysis (Peters et al., 2003).
11: This LCA study followed the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (Guide, 2006; Standardization, 2006) which recommends: (1) a clearly defined goal and purpose of the study; (2) a clearly defined scope with a functional unit, system boundary, and impact assessment methods; (3) an inventory of the data used for the analysis that can be related back to the functional unit; (4) sensi- tivity analyses; (5) categories of environmental impacts analyzed; and (6) a discussion of limitations of the analysis. A consequential modeling approach was applied to this study, as the goal was to compare the environmental impacts of using energy from poultry litter combustion for heating poultry houses and compare to LPG, which is widely used for heating poultry houses in the US, and NG, which is used in many heating applications both within and outside the US.

The LCA of the FBC system for poultry litter combustion and comparison to LPG and NG use was performed using SimaPro software (Version 9.0, PRé Sustainability, Netherlands). The envi- ronmental impacts were estimated using the Recipe 1.10/World (May 2014) midpoint (H) impact assessment method. The following 18 impact categories included in the assessment: climate change potential (CCP), ozone depletion potential (ODP), terrestrial acidi- fication potential (TAP), freshwater eutrophication potential (FEP), marine eutrophication potential (MEP), human toxicity potential (HTP), photochemical oxidant formation (POF), particulate matter formation (PMF), terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TEP), freshwater ecotoxicity potential (FETP), marine ecotoxicity potential (METP), ionizing radiation (IR), agricultural land occupation (ALO), urban land occupation (ULO), natural land transformation (NLT), metal depletion (MD), fossil depletion (FD), and water depletion (WD). The results presented focus on the CCP, TAP, FEP, and MEP cate- gories due to its relevance to renewable energy production from waste resources and the health of the Chesapeake Bay region where the study was located.

13: <header>2.4. Goal and scope of the life cycle assessment study </header>

The goal of the study was to quantify the environmental impacts of generating heat and electricity from the combustion of poultry litter. The scope was from cradle to grave, including poultry litter storage, poultry litter combustion, heat production conversion of the generated heat into electricity using the ORC system, and co- production of ash as a fertilizer, with the LCA system boundary shown in Fig. 1. Impacts of the construction materials and assembly of the FBC plant were included in the LCA (Ecoinvent version 3.0). Transport of the poultry manure to the FBC system was not included, as the FBC was located at the farm site and did not require additional transportation for use. During the study period, the poultry litter and the ash produced from the combustion process were not transported off the farm, and hence, this distance was unknown and not included in the LCA.

The impacts from the production of the poultry litter were also not included in the study, as poultry is bred for meat and eggs and not for the manure/litter. As such, the impacts associated with poultry production would be the same for all the studied scenarios (Billen et al., 2015). Two main scenarios were considered for the impact assessment:

1. The Baseline scenario (Baseline) used data from the actual operating conditions and outputs of the FBC unit monitored over the 16-month study period. During this time, the parasitic electric load for operating the FBC unit (131,072 kWh) was higher than the electricity generated, with a net negative elec- tricity output (-112,700 kWh).

2 The Improved Operational scenario (Improved) assumed the FBC system operated for 6720 h annually (77% annual runtime, instead of 30% in the Baseline), combusted 1655 tons of poultry litter annually at a 0.246 tons/h feed rate (instead of 0.176 tons/h in the Baseline), and a net positive electricity output (68,404 kWh). The parameters for annual runtime and biomass feed processing for this scenario were obtained using the manufacturer's set expectations, while the remaining parame- ters were calculated using these runtime and feed expected values. The manufacturer expected a feed rate (0.246 tons/h) 40% higher than the feed rate observed in the Baseline scenario (0.176 tons/h).

Average Maryland electricity consumption is a combination of coal, NG, and nuclear power (approximately 2:1:1 ratio) (EIA, 2017), while the heating of poultry litter houses is exclusively from LPG use. The impacts of the Baseline scenario were compared to the impacts associated with the production and use of LPG and NG for heating purposes. The functional unit for the impact assessment was 'generation of 1 MJ of energy'. The input and output system energy flows in the Baseline were based on energy produced from poultry litter combustion after 16 months of monitoring (0.184 kg poultry litter/MJ energy), with the Improved scenario (0.140 kg poultry litter/MJ energy) based on calculated values, as described above. The lifetime of the FBC system was assumed to be 20 years.

17: Emissions of carbon dioxide (CO2), carbon monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2), oxides of N (NO and NO2 combined as NOx), par- ticulate matter (PM), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from the FBC process were obtained from the emissions testing data from an independent third-party contractor using USEPA Reference test methods 1-2, 3A, 4, 5/202, 6C/7E, 10, and 25A. N20 emissions were obtained from literature data related to combustion of poultry litter in FBC systems (Billen et al., 2015). CO2 emissions were considered to be biogenic, as the organic carbon in poultry litter is of biogenic origin and the renewable energy generated can replace energy from fossil fuels (Billen et al., 2015). Methane (CH4) emis- sions from storage were assumed to be negligible (Reijnders and Huijbregts, 2005). The poultry litter storage emissions were not monitored, and literature data were used to obtain ammonia (NH3) and nitrous oxide (N20) emissions based on the %N content of poultry litter (Moore et al., 2011).

Diesel use for FBC start-up was obtained from data logs. As explained previously, the total (thermal - electrical) energy output was obtained from 16 months of data monitoring. One unit of thermal energy from poultry litter and one unit of thermal energy from LPG or NG were assumed to be equivalent, and one unit of electrical energy from poultry litter and one unit of electrical en- ergy from the grid were assumed to be equivalent. Sand use in the FBC system was obtained from literature data of a similar poultry litter FBC system in Netherlands (de Graff et al., 2017). The data on the materials used in construction were estimated by the manu- facturer. The impacts associated with NG production and use, LPG production and use, construction materials, sand use, and diesel use were obtained from the US Ecoinvent version 2.2 in SimaPro. The LCA inventory data for all the data are shown in Table 1.

19: In the sensitivity analysis, each variable of interest (electricity use for FBC operation, startup diesel for bringing the FBC system up to temperature, combustion emissions, emissions from poultry litter storage, and construction material input) was modified
Fig. 1. System boundary of the fluidized bed combustion (FBC) of poultry litter for heat and electricity production using an organic Rankine cycle (ORC) generator.

by + 10%, while keeping the other variables constant for the Baseline scenario. The percent change in environmental impact in each impact category for every variable of interest was then calculated by comparing it to the environmental impacts in the Baseline scenario.

The environmental burdens for all the 18 impact categories associated with the two scenarios tested in this study (Baseline Scenario and Improved Scenario) were analyzed for significant differences using a t-test for two independent means with an alpha value set at 0.05 (Hijazi et al., 2020). The means and standard de- viations of the impact categories for the two scenarios were calculated using SimaPro software (Version 9.0, PRé Sustainability, Netherlands).

21: 3.1. Energy production from the fluidized bed combustion system

The total energy supplied to the two poultry houses for heating the six flocks in 16 months was 1,504,727 kWh, which included energy from the FBC unit (858,569 kWh) and energy from back up diesel use when the FBC unit was not operational (646,158 kWh). The FBC operated for a total of 3226 h and combusted 568 tons of poultry litter, with an average feed rate of 0.176 tons/h. The elec- trical energy production ranged from 0.7 to 4.0% of the total energy produced per flock, with a cumulative electrical energy production of 12,527 kWh during the study period, as most of the potential energy from the poultry litter was used for heating the houses and not delivered to the ORC.

The energy efficiency of the FBC system was consistent, with an average of 55% of the calorific value of the poultry litter (9983 + 251 kJ/kg) converted into total energy, with an average energy output of 1534 kWh/ton poultry litter combusted. Under the Improved scenario, the LCA was conducted assuming the FBC sys- tem operated for 6720 h/y (77% annual runtime), and utilized 1655 tons poultry litter/year at a 0.246 tons/h feed rate, resulting in an

energy production efficiency of 67% and a biomass to energy con- version efficiency of 1985 kWh/ton poultry litter combusted. In practical applications of small-scale biomass fueled ORC systems, the thermal energy production efficiency can vary from 60 to 70%, with 15% efficiency in electricity production (Cotana et al., 2014).
22: 3.1. Energy production from the fluidized bed combustion system

The total energy supplied to the two poultry houses for heating the six flocks in 16 months was 1,504,727 kWh, which included energy from the FBC unit (858,569 kWh) and energy from back up diesel use when the FBC unit was not operational (646,158 kWh). The FBC operated for a total of 3226 h and combusted 568 tons of poultry litter, with an average feed rate of 0.176 tons/h. The elec- trical energy production ranged from 0.7 to 4.0% of the total energy produced per flock, with a cumulative electrical energy production of 12,527 kWh during the study period, as most of the potential energy from the poultry litter was used for heating the houses and not delivered to the ORC.

The energy efficiency of the FBC system was consistent, with an average of 55% of the calorific value of the poultry litter (9983 + 251 kJ/kg) converted into total energy, with an average energy output of 1534 kWh/ton poultry litter combusted. Under the Improved scenario, the LCA was conducted assuming the FBC sys- tem operated for 6720 h/y (77% annual runtime), and utilized 1655 tons poultry litter/year at a 0.246 tons/h feed rate, resulting in an

energy production efficiency of 67% and a biomass to energy con- version efficiency of 1985 kWh/ton poultry litter combusted. In practical applications of small-scale biomass fueled ORC systems, the thermal energy production efficiency can vary from 60 to 70%, with 15% efficiency in electricity production (Cotana et al., 2014).
23: The climate change potential of poultry litter combustion cor- responded to 0.053 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy, which was 32% and 44% less than the CCP associated with LPG and NG production and use, respectively (0.078 and 0.095 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy, respectively) (Table 2, Fig. 2). When converted to a mass basis, the total CCP impact due to GHG emissions (N20 and CO), corresponding to the combustion of 1 kg of poultry litter, was 0.0775 kg CO2 eq/kg poultry litter combusted. The N20 emissions from the combustion process (0.260 g/kg poultry litter) and storage emissions (0.070 g/ kg poultry litter) had the highest contribution to CCP, as N20 is 298 times more potent GHG than CO2. The CO emissions (0.750 g/kg poultry litter) had a minimal impact on CCP.

The impact of combusting poultry manure on climate change was a combination of direct emissions of GHGs, upstream emis- sions originating from the construction and assembly of the plant, emissions associated with storage of poultry litter, start-up diesel use, and electricity required for daily operation of the FBC system. It was expected that the FBC system would produce excess electricity, especially during the summer when heating requirements for the poultry houses were lower. One reason for the reduced electric production was the presence of rocks and other foreign materials in poultry litter feedstock in the US that were not present on UK/EU farms and caused damage to the unit built for UK/EU conditions. The US feedstock also had a lower calorific value (9983 kJ/kg) and higher moisture content (39%) compared to the UK/EU feedstock (moisture content ~ 25%; calorific value ~ 13,000 kJ/kg) (Quiroga et al., 2010). Studies have recommended that the moisture con- tent of poultry litter should not exceed 25% for efficient heat and electrical energy generation (Dalólio et al., 2017).
Due to the system not performing at its highest efficiency throughout most of the study period, the electricity produced was

The increased thermal energy output avoided impacts on CCP corresponding to 0.074 kg CO2 eq/ MJ and 0.093 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy due to the replacement of thermal energy from LPG and NG production and use, respectively (Table 3, Fig. 3).

5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

not sufficient to offset the parasitic load required for daily operation of the system. As a result, the net electricity input had the highest impact on CCP (53%) for the combustion of poultry litter. The electricity input accounted for GHG emissions of 0.028 kg CO2 eq/ MJ of energy. Emissions associated with poultry litter storage and start-up diesel use contributed to 7.3% and 8.9% of the GHG emis- sions, respectively. The study showed that the poultry litter quality, poultry litter combustion value, and litter management need to be carefully considered before system design and installation to ensure efficient performance.
In the Improved scenario, the combustion process had 66% less

impact (0.018 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy) on CCP than the Baseline due to the increased biomass feed rate, yearly run-time, and energy output/biomass feed ratio. As a result, the amount of poultry litter required to produce 1 MJ of energy decreased from 0.184 kg to 0.140 kg in the Improved scenario. The increased thermal energy output avoided impacts on CCP corresponding to 0.074 kg CO2 eq/ MJ and 0.093 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy due to the replacement of thermal energy from LPG and NG production and use, respectively (Table 3, Fig. 3).

The increased thermal energy output avoided impacts on CCP corresponding to 0.074 kg CO2 eq/ MJ and 0.093 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy due to the replacement of thermal energy from LPG and NG production and use, respectively (Table 3, Fig. 3).

5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.

5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

As a result, the net electricity input had the highest impact on CCP (53%) for the combustion of poultry litter. The electricity input accounted for GHG emissions of 0.028 kg CO2 eq/ MJ of energy. Emissions associated with poultry litter storage and start-up diesel use contributed to 7.3% and 8.9% of the GHG emis- sions, respectively. The study showed that the poultry litter quality, poultry litter combustion value, and litter management need to be carefully considered before system design and installation to ensure efficient performance.
In the Improved scenario, the combustion process had 66% less

impact (0.018 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy) on CCP than the Baseline due to the increased biomass feed rate, yearly run-time, and energy output/biomass feed ratio. As a result, the amount of poultry litter required to produce 1 MJ of energy decreased from 0.184 kg to 0.140 kg in the Improved scenario. The increased thermal energy output avoided impacts on CCP corresponding to 0.074 kg CO2 eq/ MJ and 0.093 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy due to the replacement of thermal energy from LPG and NG production and use, respectively (Table 3, Fig. 3).

The increased thermal energy output avoided impacts on CCP corresponding to 0.074 kg CO2 eq/ MJ and 0.093 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy due to the replacement of thermal energy from LPG and NG production and use, respectively (Table 3, Fig. 3).

5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
