5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

As a result, the net electricity input had the highest impact on CCP (53%) for the combustion of poultry litter. The electricity input accounted for GHG emissions of 0.028 kg CO2 eq/ MJ of energy. Emissions associated with poultry litter storage and start-up diesel use contributed to 7.3% and 8.9% of the GHG emis- sions, respectively. The study showed that the poultry litter quality, poultry litter combustion value, and litter management need to be carefully considered before system design and installation to ensure efficient performance.
In the Improved scenario, the combustion process had 66% less

impact (0.018 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy) on CCP than the Baseline due to the increased biomass feed rate, yearly run-time, and energy output/biomass feed ratio. As a result, the amount of poultry litter required to produce 1 MJ of energy decreased from 0.184 kg to 0.140 kg in the Improved scenario. The increased thermal energy output avoided impacts on CCP corresponding to 0.074 kg CO2 eq/ MJ and 0.093 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy due to the replacement of thermal energy from LPG and NG production and use, respectively (Table 3, Fig. 3).

The increased thermal energy output avoided impacts on CCP corresponding to 0.074 kg CO2 eq/ MJ and 0.093 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy due to the replacement of thermal energy from LPG and NG production and use, respectively (Table 3, Fig. 3).

5.5% of the total energy produced in this scenario. As a result, the FBC system had a net electrical energy output of 68,404 kWh (after parasitic load subtraction). The excess renewable electricity led to avoided impacts on CCP from the production of electricity from fossil fuel sources (0.005 kg CO2 eq/MJ of energy). The contribution of combustion emissions to CCP was the highest in the Improved scenario (61%), followed by start-up diesel use (20%), and emissions from storage (16%).

In the present study, the avoided impacts on CCP in the Baseline scenario were associated with offsetting thermal energy from LPG and NG corresponding to 133 g and 226 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. In the Improved scenario, the reduction of impacts on CCP for poultry litter combustion were 2.5-3.3 higher and corre- sponded to 437 and 567 CO2 eq/kg poultry litter for LPG and NG, respectively. In the study conducted by Billen et al. (2015), the avoided impacts on CCP were associated with offsetting electrical energy, instead of thermal energy, from coal and NG, with re- ductions of 655 g and 357 g CO2 eq/kg poultry litter, respectively. Williams et al. (2016) found a net 3% decrease in CCP from burning turkey litter for electricity production compared to the use of NG, which is lower than the present study (44% decrease in the Baseline

scenario, 82% decrease in the Improved scenario). However, they included the loss of soil carbon from not applying the litter on land in their analysis and suggested that this led to the net effect being minimal. Billen et al. (2015) argued that the loss of soil carbon should not have a negative impact, as carbon fertilization may not be necessary in most cases, and carbon neutral sources for increasing organic matter content, such as compost, are easily available at low prices.
3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.

3.3. Life cycle assessment impact: eutrophication and terrestrial acidification potential

The ReCiPe model makes the assumption that marine eutro- phication potential (MEP) is most affected by the leaching of different forms of N into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophica- tion potential (FEP) is attributed to the leaching of different forms of P. Gaseous species of N, such as NH3 and NOx, have lower impacts on MEP but have the highest impact on Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), along with SOx. The Chesapeake Bay is not considered to be fresh or marine, but a brackish water body, so both FEP and MEP are highlighted below.

In the Baseline scenario, electricity usage from the grid had the highest impact on MEP kg N eq/MJ of energy), followed by NOx emissions from the combustion process kg Neq/ MJ of energy), and NH3 emissions from poultry litter storage (2.95 kg N eq/MJ of energy) (Table 2, Fig. 2). FEP was primarily affected by the material use for the FBC system (79%) followed by electricity use for its operation (21%). The overall TAP corresponded to 4.1 X kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy, with electricity use for FBC operation having the highest contribution (64%), followed by NH3 emissions from storage (19%), and NOx and SOX emissions from the combustion process (11%).

Poultry litter has low amounts of S (0.71%) and up to 90% of S present in poultry litter can react with the oxides of Ca produced during the combustion process to form Ca-S complexes (Dalólio et al., 2017). As a result, the emission of SOx from poultry litter combustion (0.005 g/kg poultry litter) is significantly lower than emissions from coal combustion (0.830 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015; Kelleher et al., 2002). The flue gas from the combus- tion process also had lower concentrations of NO (0.42 g/kg poultry litter) compared to coal combustion (1.1 g/kg poultry litter), but more than the emissions from NG combustion (0.34 g/kg poultry litter) (Billen et al., 2015). However, due to the electricity requirement to operate the FBC unit, the total TAP (4.1 X SO2 eq/MJ of energy) and MEP (1.1 X kg N eq/MJ of energy) of the combustion process had 69% and 80% higher impacts, respectively, when compared to LPG production and usage (1.3 X kg SO2 eq/ MJ of energy and 2.4 X kg N eq/MJ of energy, respectively), resulting in a net negative environmental impact (Fig. 3). The impact of NG production and usage in MEP kg eq/MJ of energy) was 74% lower than the combustion process, but its impact on TAP (6.7 X 10-6 kg N eq/MJ of energy) was 38% higher.

In the Improved scenario, TAP and MEP were reduced by 75% and 58%, respectively, compared to the Baseline scenario. As a result, a net environmental benefit of 6.1 X 10-5 kg SO2 eq/MJ of energy was seen in TAP when compared to LPG use, but the increased efficiencies were not able to offset the impacts on MEP due to the volatilization of NH3 from storage and NO emissions from the combustion process (Table 3, Fig. 3). Freshwater eutro- phication potential was 31% and 11% higher in the Baseline scenario compared to the use of LPG and NG, respectively. However, due to the improved efficiencies and a net positive electrical energy pro- duction in the Improved scenario, the avoided impacts from elec- tricity use from the grid, LPG and NG use resulted in lowering FEP impacts for the FBC combustion process by >75%.
44: <header>3.4. Other life cycle assessment environmental impact categories </header>

The results for the other environmental categories in the Base- line scenario (Fig. 2A) show that the electricity use for plant oper- ation and the material use for the FBC unit were the main contributors to all the impacts, except for PMF. Combustion of poultry litter in the FBC unit had the highest contribution towards PMF due to emissions of particulate matter <10 um, SO2, and NOx. Human toxicity potential was most affected by the material use and the start-up diesel use for bringing the FBC to its optimum tem- perature. Ozone depletion potential was also affected by the ma- terial use due to the use of a fluorocarbon refrigerant in the heat exchanger system, but emissions are expected to be negligible during the lifetime of the system due to the airtight enclosure surrounding the heat exchanger system. As expected, steel and aluminum use in the construction of the FBC unit had the highest contribution towards MD (89%). When compared to the avoided production and use of LPG for heat production, a net environmental gain was only observed in 6 (CCP, ODP, TEP, IRP, NLT, and FD) out of the 18 impact categories. In contrast, a net environmental gain was

observed in 11 out of the 18 categories when the combustion process (Baseline) was compared to the production and use of NG. In the Improved scenario, the material usage in the construction of the FBC unit had the highest contribution in 9 out of the 18 impact categories, followed by start-up diesel use in 4 out of the 18 categories (Fig. 3A). The reduction in all other environmental im- pacts due to the improved efficiencies in the Improved scenario ranged from 48 to 98% when compared to the actual conditions in the Baseline scenario. Due to the higher electricity production in the Improved scenario, a net environmental gain was observed in 14 out of the 18 impact categories when compared to the use of LPG, indicating the necessity for improved efficiencies for heat and electricity production when operating the FBC unit. In addition, the net environmental gains were observed in 17 out of the 18 impact categories in the Improved scenario were compared to the use of NG.

46: In the Baseline scenario, LPG was observed to be the most environmentally sustainable energy source, followed by poultry litter and NG. The freshwater ecotoxicity potential (FETP) was the only category where NG had lower environmental impact than both LPG and poultry litter. The impacts of using NG was higher than LPG in the CCP, TAP, FEP, and MEP impact categories (19%, 76%, 23%, and 19% higher, respectively). NG resulted in lower environ- mental impacts than LPG in 4 of the 18 impact factors (ODP, FETP, IRP, and NLT). In the Improved scenario, however, the use of poultry litter combustion was able to offset the environmental impacts for 17 out of the 18 impact categories when compared to NG use. Marine eutrophication potential was the only impact category where the use of NG resulted in lower environmental impacts (by 23%) compared to poultry litter combustion. The analysis showed that the choice of the heating fuel used for comparison to poultry litter combustion is important to gauge the sustainability of the process. Even with the poor performance of the FBC system in the Baseline scenario, it was able to offset the environmental impacts associated with NG use for heating in 11 out of the 18 impact categories.
47: The study results focused on the CCP, TAP, FEP, and MEP cate- gories due to its relevance of these categories to the health of the Chesapeake Bay, and therefore, the statistical analysis presented here focused on these four categories. The statistical analysis showed that the CCP, TAP, FEP, and MEP impacts for the Improved Scenario were significantly lower than the Baseline Scenario (p- value <0.0001 for all four categories).
48: The effects of changing the electricity input for running the FBC system (+10%) resulted in the highest average (4.8%) change in the impact category values, while the emissions from the litter storage had the lowest average change (0.4%) (Fig. 4). The litter was only stored for two weeks before being utilized for energy generation, leading to comparatively lower N20 and NH3 emissions. The 10% change in electrical energy input resulted in a 7.9% change in FD due to the use of coal and NG for electricity production in Maryland. Both the combustion emissions from the FBC process and electrical energy input resulted in a 5.3% change in CCP, which was the highest impact out of all the impact categories tested. The material of construction had the highest impact on metal depletion, as ex- pected. Freshwater eutrophication potential was also primarily
Fig. 4. Percent change in impact categories upon 10% change in the five variables tested for the sensitivity analysis.

affected (7.9% change) by the material of construction, while MEP was equally impacted by the electrical energy input and the com- bustion emissions containing N. As previously discussed, the ReCiPe model attributes marine eutrophication to different forms of N emissions into the ocean, while freshwater eutrophication is attributed to different forms of P. The sensitivity analysis provided support to the notion that improved efficiencies for heat and electricity production would lead to a more sustainable process, as it would eliminate the impacts associated with the electrical energy input for FBC operation.

50: The use of poultry litter as a fertilizer avoids the production of N, P, and K fertilizer that have significant environmental impacts in all impact categories. While the use of poultry litter ash can be a substitute for P and K fertilizers, it cannot offset the environmental burdens associated with inorganic N fertilizer production and us- age. Due to the uncertainties associated with the use of poultry litter directly or poultry litter ash as a fertilizer, it was excluded from the impact assessment, and the focus of the study was directed towards the impacts associated with the combustion process only. However, it is important to consider the implications of land application of the poultry litter and its effect on the sus- tainability of alternative disposal technologies for poultry litter need to be discussed.

While several studies have been conducted on alternative disposal technologies for poultry litter, peer-reviewed articles on LCA of these technologies are limited. Two peer reviewed studies conducted LCA analyses on poultry litter combustion and turkey litter combustion, and a 2019 study conducted an LCA on poultry litter gasification (Billen et al., 2015; Jeswani et al., 2019; Williams et al., 2016). All authors concluded that combustion and gasification of poultry litter were attractive alternatives to land application, especially in areas with high concentration of poultry farms and restrictions imposed on direct land application of the litter. How- ever, it is difficult to ascertain if such alternative waste disposal technologies can lead to environmental benefits if poultry farms were more evenly dispersed. Concentrated operations can lead to over-application and increased nutrient concentration in soil,

which could be alleviated through manure combustion, gasifica- tion, and pyrolysis processes (Williams et al., 2016). Poultry litter application directly on land that is deficient in nutrients, such as N and P, may be the preferred mode of disposal, as poultry litter application can offset the use of inorganic fertilizers. In addition, poultry litter increases soil organic matter, leading to improvement of both the physical and chemical soil attributes (Dalólio et al., 2017).

Unmanaged and excessive agronomic utilization of poultry litter can result in eutrophication of water bodies, spread of pathogens, and GHG emissions (Billen et al., 2015). The primary pathways for these environmental concerns include leaching losses of soluble P, NH4, and NO3 to water bodies, and emissions of N20 and NO (Szogi et al., 2019). In addition, NH3 volatilization also results in N losses, especially within the first two weeks of land application (Moore et al., 1995). A previous study reported that NH3 losses from poultry litter can vary, in the range of 20-45% of total ammonia N (TAN), while another study reported a loss of 24% of total N in the form on NH3 when the poultry litter was mixed with sandy soils (Szogi et al., 2019; Meisinger and Jokela, 2000). However, the percent loss decreased to 1-8% of the total N when the litter was incorporated into the soil. The factors that determine the loss of NH3 from land application of manure are dependent on manure characteristics, application management, soil conditions, and environmental factors (Szogi et al., 2019).

Leaching losses of soluble P, NH4, and NO3 to water bodies also depend on several parameters, as described above for NH3 emis- sions to air. As a result, obtaining emission data for land spreading of manure is not straightforward and subject to large uncertainties. Additionally, quantification of the emissions associated with the replacement of inorganic fertilizer can be uncertain and would need to be incorporated into the model. Ammonia emissions associated with inorganic fertilizer use are dependent on the amount and type of fertilizer, soil pH, wind speed, and application method, while the N20 and NO emissions are dependent on the water-filled pore space, among other factors (Akiyama et al., 2004). It should be noted that poultry litter ash has several advantages over untreated manure due to its value as a P and K fertilizer, including the fact that the ash is 75%-90% less bulky, which makes it easier to transport (Billen et al., 2015; Codling, 2006). The absence

of N in the ash prevents harmful gaseous emissions of NH3 and N20, while also allowing for its application separately based on only the P needs of the soil, thus, reducing potential P run-off that could result from the application of the litter on a N requirement basis for the crop. Furthermore, the low percentage of water-soluble inor- ganic P (1.5%) in the ash compared to raw poultry litter (55%) could potentially lead to reduced run-off when used as a fertilizer or soil amendment (Ma et al., 2019). Even with the low concentration of water-soluble P, the ash can be an effective P fertilizer for crops (Ma et al., 2019).

The LCA does not take pathogen destruction into account. Poultry manure may cause diseases due to emissions of pathogens into air and water after land application (Billen et al., 2015; Moore et al., 1995). Even though land application of poultry litter has rarely been associated with foodborne outbreaks, the combustion process does produce a pathogen-free ash product and flue gases due to temperatures in excess of 850 °C inside the furnace. This should also be taken into consideration when alternative poultry litter management technologies are analyzed for sustainability, espe- cially in areas with a high concentration of poultry farms (Chen and Jiang, 2014).

The primary pathways for these environmental concerns include leaching losses of soluble P, NH4, and NO3 to water bodies, and emissions of N20 and NO (Szogi et al., 2019). In addition, NH3 volatilization also results in N losses, especially within the first two weeks of land application (Moore et al., 1995). A previous study reported that NH3 losses from poultry litter can vary, in the range of 20-45% of total ammonia N (TAN), while another study reported a loss of 24% of total N in the form on NH3 when the poultry litter was mixed with sandy soils (Szogi et al., 2019; Meisinger and Jokela, 2000). However, the percent loss decreased to 1-8% of the total N when the litter was incorporated into the soil. The factors that determine the loss of NH3 from land application of manure are dependent on manure characteristics, application management, soil conditions, and environmental factors (Szogi et al., 2019).

Leaching losses of soluble P, NH4, and NO3 to water bodies also depend on several parameters, as described above for NH3 emis- sions to air. As a result, obtaining emission data for land spreading of manure is not straightforward and subject to large uncertainties. Additionally, quantification of the emissions associated with the replacement of inorganic fertilizer can be uncertain and would need to be incorporated into the model. Ammonia emissions associated with inorganic fertilizer use are dependent on the amount and type of fertilizer, soil pH, wind speed, and application method, while the N20 and NO emissions are dependent on the water-filled pore space, among other factors (Akiyama et al., 2004). It should be noted that poultry litter ash has several advantages over untreated manure due to its value as a P and K fertilizer, including the fact that the ash is 75%-90% less bulky, which makes it easier to transport (Billen et al., 2015; Codling, 2006). The absence

of N in the ash prevents harmful gaseous emissions of NH3 and N20, while also allowing for its application separately based on only the P needs of the soil, thus, reducing potential P run-off that could result from the application of the litter on a N requirement basis for the crop. Furthermore, the low percentage of water-soluble inor- ganic P (1.5%) in the ash compared to raw poultry litter (55%) could potentially lead to reduced run-off when used as a fertilizer or soil amendment (Ma et al., 2019). Even with the low concentration of water-soluble P, the ash can be an effective P fertilizer for crops (Ma et al., 2019).

The LCA does not take pathogen destruction into account. Poultry manure may cause diseases due to emissions of pathogens into air and water after land application (Billen et al., 2015; Moore et al., 1995). Even though land application of poultry litter has rarely been associated with foodborne outbreaks, the combustion process does produce a pathogen-free ash product and flue gases due to temperatures in excess of 850 °C inside the furnace. This should also be taken into consideration when alternative poultry litter management technologies are analyzed for sustainability, espe- cially in areas with a high concentration of poultry farms (Chen and Jiang, 2014).
55: The use of poultry litter as a fertilizer avoids the production of N, P, and K fertilizer that have significant environmental impacts in all impact categories. While the use of poultry litter ash can be a substitute for P and K fertilizers, it cannot offset the environmental burdens associated with inorganic N fertilizer production and us- age. Due to the uncertainties associated with the use of poultry litter directly or poultry litter ash as a fertilizer, it was excluded from the impact assessment, and the focus of the study was directed towards the impacts associated with the combustion process only. However, it is important to consider the implications of land application of the poultry litter and its effect on the sus- tainability of alternative disposal technologies for poultry litter need to be discussed.

While several studies have been conducted on alternative disposal technologies for poultry litter, peer-reviewed articles on LCA of these technologies are limited. Two peer reviewed studies conducted LCA analyses on poultry litter combustion and turkey litter combustion, and a 2019 study conducted an LCA on poultry litter gasification (Billen et al., 2015; Jeswani et al., 2019; Williams et al., 2016). All authors concluded that combustion and gasification of poultry litter were attractive alternatives to land application, especially in areas with high concentration of poultry farms and restrictions imposed on direct land application of the litter. How- ever, it is difficult to ascertain if such alternative waste disposal technologies can lead to environmental benefits if poultry farms were more evenly dispersed. Concentrated operations can lead to over-application and increased nutrient concentration in soil,

which could be alleviated through manure combustion, gasifica- tion, and pyrolysis processes (Williams et al., 2016). Poultry litter application directly on land that is deficient in nutrients, such as N and P, may be the preferred mode of disposal, as poultry litter application can offset the use of inorganic fertilizers. 57: The study focused on the FBC combustion process and did take into consideration emissions associated with the land application of the poultry litter and the ash byproduct. One of the most important results from the analysis was that the use of this tech- nology by itself can help reduce impacts on climate change po- tential, but a more complete analysis of the impacts on land application would provide a better understanding on how the process can affect climate change. It is also important to note that the LCA was conducted on a demonstration full-scale project that did not function as expected. While the study incorporated the Baseline and Improved scenarios, each FBC system will have vari- ations in total energy production and operational efficiencies that would vary from the two scenarios analyzed in this study. For example, a full-scale poultry litter combustion system in Netherlands processed 438,000 tons of poultry litter annually and produced 582 kWh/ton poultry litter of electrical energy and 137 kWh/ton poultry litter of thermal energy at a net efficiency of 29% (de Graff et al., 2017). Another plant in Scotland, UK combusted 110,000 tons/y of poultry litter to produce 780 kWh/ton of electrical energy using fluidized bed technology (Florin et al., 2009). In the Baseline scenario for the current study, the thermal energy pro- duction was 1511 kWh/ton poultry litter and the electrical energy production was 23 kWh/ton poultry litter at a net efficiency of 55%. Currently, poultry litter combustion systems are commercialized in Europe and the UK, with previous studies discussing its benefits, but LCA studies have disagreed on the best way to incorporate the impacts of land application. Additionally, there are very few poultry litter combustion systems operating in the US, with most of these limited systems operating at the pilot-scale. The study did not consider decommissioning and disposal of the plant materials. Recycling these materials may significantly reduce impacts in the material depletion category.
58: The LCA of the FBC system showed net environmental gains from combusting poultry litter for heating poultry houses and producing renewable electricity, especially in the climate change potential category. In the Baseline scenario, using the FBC for heating offset the environmental impacts associated with LPG use in only 6 out of the 18 impact categories. However, during 16-

month monitoring period, the FBC system did not function opti- mally, with lower than expected biomass feed rate, operating hours, and energy output/biomass feed ratio, resulting in more electric input to the FBC system than electricity generation. The FBC unit was impacted by differences in poultry litter characteristics in the US compared to UK/EU FBC system design, as the varying moisture content of the litter and the increased presence of foreign matter in the litter interfered with the combustion process. An Improved operational scenario was calculated (77% annual run- time, 0.246 tons/h feed rate, and 68,404 kWh net positive elec- tricity production) and showed significantly lower environmental impacts compared to the Baseline (30% annual runtime, 0.176 tons/ h feed rate and 112,700 kWh net negative electricity production), which highlights the need for a net positive electricity output during FBC operation to increase the sustainability of the process. In the Improved scenario, the use of poultry litter had lower impacts than both LPG and NG, with NG having the highest impacts. Replacement of NG with poultry litter will have the largest sus- tainability benefits. Additional benefits of the FBC process include the production of a dry and odorless ash without volatile N emis- sions. The lower weight of the ash product compared to the original litter decreases transport costs of the fertilizer to regions with deficient soil P concentrations. Currently, Maryland, US, has a transportation program for poultry litter due to the high P-value of the litter and excess soil P in areas of high poultry production. The FBC system would reduce the costs of the litter transport and may be especially important in areas where restrictions have been imposed on land application of the litter. This study emphasized the need to evaluate the poultry litter quality, litter management, replacement fuel source, and electricity production potential before project commencement to prevent sub-optimal operational conditions.
